DockRoom.entry: Clanker City! The big smoke! The city of ten thousand gears!
One small maintenance-bot, one dream, and not so much as an oil can to my name.
Thanks for the ride! I'll catch the return ferry once I'm rich and famous.
Well. Guess I live here now.
TavernRoom.entry: The Scrap & Barrel. Where the floor is sticky and nothing is explained.
The bartender says to mind the everything.
The piano is playing itself. Badly. With confidence.
AlleyRoom.entry: The alley behind the Scrap & Barrel. Where the city keeps its rough drafts.
There's a gate at the end marked MIDTOWN, going up. Uptown. The future.
MidtownRoom.entry: Midtown Gearworks. The neon is louder, the streets are cleaner, and the manholes hiss in a higher register.
City Hall says NOTHING IS WRONG in letters you can read from the harbor.
And up the hill, behind the fence: the Great Dynamo. You can hear it from here.
It's flat. By half a step.

LookAt: WELCOME TO CLANKER CITY. POP. 8,01,1.
Population eight-oh-one-comma-one. The punctuation died first.
Use: Maybe a good honest whack will fix the display.
It rained a bolt. In this town that's basically a tip.
It already gave me everything it had.
Move: Maybe a good honest whack will fix the display.
It rained a bolt. In this town that's basically a tip.
It already gave me everything it had.
Smell: Ozone and civic neglect.

LookAt: One genuine Clanker City bolt. Legal tender, light snack, structural member.
PickUp: One bolt. Only eight thousand and ten more and I can afford a personality upgrade.
Use: Those two things politely decline each other.
I'm saving it. It's one ninth of a fortune.
Smell: Mmm. Zinc-plated.

LookAt: The S.S. Eventually. My ride here, and apparently nothing's ride anywhere ever again.
Use: The engine made a noise like a dying accordion and is now doing modern art.
Open: The engine made a noise like a dying accordion and is now doing modern art.
TalkTo: Hang in there, buddy.
The foghorn wheezed. I'm choosing to hear that as optimism.
Smell: Salt water and burnt transmission fluid. The smell of adventure!

LookAt: A cargo crane, frozen mid-lift. Or mid-yawn. Hard to tell.
The operator plate says 'BOOM-ARM BETTY'. Her eye-light is blinking in morse.
O... I... L. Yeah, Betty, we've all been there.
Betty's lifting things and putting them down again. For joy, near as I can tell.
TalkTo: Hello up there! Lovely evening!
She'd wave back if she could. She's out of oil, not out of manners.
Her eye-light blinks: T... H... X.
She's billing by the letter. I respect it.
Use: Her joints are dry as a tax form. She needs oil before she'll move that crate.
UsedWith: Open wide, Betty. Doctor's orders.
The oil goes in. Twenty years of squeak go out.
Betty sets the crate down with the gentleness of a very large aunt.
Her eye-light blinks T-H-X. We're friends now. It's official.
She considers it from up there and blinks a polite no.
Smell: Burnt servo grease. A working robot's perfume.

LookAt: Stenciled on the side: PROPERTY OF THE ORDER OF THE WIND-UP KEY.
Ominous AND conveniently plot-relevant. Also twenty feet up.
On the dock now, at a polite opening height.
Empty, except for packing grease and destiny residue.
PickUp: It's twenty feet up. My arms are stock parts.
Betty could set it down, if anyone gave her a drink of oil.
The lid argues. I argue back with a plank.
A brass wind-up key the size of my forearm. One of three, if the stencil is honest.
Act One: complete. Probably. Towns like this love a twist.
Already opened. The destiny has been extracted.
Use: It's twenty feet up. My arms are stock parts.
Betty could set it down, if anyone gave her a drink of oil.
The lid argues. I argue back with a plank.
A brass wind-up key the size of my forearm. One of three, if the stencil is honest.
Act One: complete. Probably. Towns like this love a twist.
Already opened. The destiny has been extracted.
Move: It's twenty feet up. My arms are stock parts.
Betty could set it down, if anyone gave her a drink of oil.
The lid argues. I argue back with a plank.
A brass wind-up key the size of my forearm. One of three, if the stencil is honest.
Act One: complete. Probably. Towns like this love a twist.
Already opened. The destiny has been extracted.
Open: It's twenty feet up. My arms are stock parts.
Betty could set it down, if anyone gave her a drink of oil.
The lid argues. I argue back with a plank.
A brass wind-up key the size of my forearm. One of three, if the stencil is honest.
Act One: complete. Probably. Towns like this love a twist.
Already opened. The destiny has been extracted.

LookAt: Rivet's Scrap Emporium: 'One bot's trash is this bot's entire business model.'
The shutter sign says: BACK IN FIVE OIL CHANGES.
Open: Shuttered tighter than the city budget.
TalkTo: HELLO? ANYONE IN THERE?
The echo has rust in it.

LookAt: Official city notice, hot off the press:
'DYNAMO MAINTENANCE: NOTHING IS WRONG. NOTHING HAS EVER BEEN WRONG.'
'DO NOT STOCKPILE OIL. SIGNED, MAYOR PISTON.'
The Great Dynamo powers every bot on this island. Including me, as of today.
So that notice is the scariest thing I've ever read.
Still nothing wrong. Officially.

PickUp: Let me read it first. I'm a maintenance-bot, not a barbarian.
Evidence. Or wall art. Possibly both.
Use: Let me read it first. I'm a maintenance-bot, not a barbarian.
Evidence. Or wall art. Possibly both.
LookAt: \"DYNAMO MAINTENANCE: NOTHING IS WRONG. - Mayor Piston.\"
Nothing says 'something is wrong' like an official notice that nothing is wrong.
Smell: Fresh ink. Suspiciously fresh.

LookAt: The Scrap & Barrel. I can hear a player piano in there, missing several keys.
...That feels thematically significant.
Move: A voice inside: 'IT'S OPEN. THE KNOCKING IS DECORATIVE.'
Smell: Spilled 10W-40 and broken dreams. So... a tavern.

LookAt: Something glints down there. Several somethings.
The city's loose change goes to the sea. Poetic. Infuriating.
Nothing glints down there anymore. You're welcome, drain.
PickUp: My fingers are factory-standard. I'd need something magnetic. On a string.
Use: My fingers are factory-standard. I'd need something magnetic. On a string.
Open: My fingers are factory-standard. I'd need something magnetic. On a string.
UsedWith: Magnet on a string. The harbor's least regulated financial instrument.
Something bites. Several somethings.
Nine bolts. This city has been tipping the ocean for years.
The drain has been fished to commercial extinction.
The drain only wants what everyone wants. Something magnetic.

LookAt: A gap between the tavern and the cliff. The sign says ALLEY, in case the dark didn't read as one.
Smell: Trouble, marinating.

LookAt: The way back to the docks. The night air smells of opportunity and brine.
SCRAP & BARREL. The ampersand is the freshest thing in here.
Smell: Neon doesn't smell. The regret underneath it does.

LookAt: Top-shelf lubricants. Literally. The bottom shelf is the same stuff with dust on it.
PickUp: Gusket's eye is tracking me like a security camera.
Because it is one. There's a little red light.
Smell: Esters, additives, and a 2% hint of regret. A good year.

LookAt: Polished to a mirror finish in one specific spot. Wonder why.
Use: I'd order something, but my warranty has opinions about additives.
Smell: Spilled 10W-40, wiped with a rag that has seen things.

LookAt: Gusket the bartender. He's been polishing that one glass since before I was assembled.
His arm servo is stuck in a loop. The glass, to be fair, is immaculate.
Gusket, finally polishing a DIFFERENT glass. Growth.
TalkTo: He says I look like I've had a hard drive, pal.
I ask about oil. He says oil is for paying customers and his last can rode off with the dart league.
He'd shake my hand, he says, but his arm's been polishing this glass since the Dynamo dimmed.
The servo's jammed. A little shim in the ratchet would free it. Something small. Something... bolt-shaped.
He says Flange by the dartboard has the last oil can in the building.
And that Flange would bet his own head gasket on a dart game.
He says my drinks are free for life. Well. One drink. Per life.
UsedWith: Hold still, sir. This is a certified maintenance procedure.
The bolt slots into the ratchet. The servo unwinds. The glass, at long last, comes down.
My fortune. Reduced to eight thousand and nine.
He flips me a drink token. Says Flange takes those as table stakes at the dartboard.
He polishes it politely and hands it back.
Smell: Bartender oil and quiet patience.

LookAt: A player piano, playing itself. It's missing three keys.
...That feels thematically significant.
Use: Two notes work. The third key isn't missing a note. It's missing entirely.
I requested a song. It requested a tuner.
Smell: Felt, brass, and the ghost of a B-flat.

LookAt: Three corroded pirate-bots playing cards for washers.
One of them insists washers are 'post-bolt currency'. The other two are holding washers, so they're not arguing hard.
TalkTo: I lean in casually. Extremely casually. Nobody has ever leaned this casually.
The one in the hat says: you CLANG twice, then once, then twice, you barnacle.
I am definitely not supposed to know that.
They've moved on to arguing about whether the table is winning.
Smell: Salt corrosion and bluffing.

LookAt: Regulation dartboard. The wall around it has suffered.
Use: Flange owns the board. Flange owns the darts. The arrangement is explained to me with gestures.
Retired champion. I don't need to prove anything.

LookAt: A dockworker bot named Flange. Arm like a hydraulic press. Currently between games.
TalkTo: He says: darts, table stakes one drink token, house rules, he always wins.
The oil can on his belt is the wager. The smugness comes free.
He's explaining to the wall how he was robbed. The wall agrees.
Give: Token on the rail. Game on.
Dart three is in the ocean. We're indoors. Nobody knows.
Flange is laughing so hard he goes for the showboat finish: three darts, one throw, behind the back.
His shoulder servo overheats with a noise like bacon achieving liftoff.
House rules: the overheated party forfeits the wager. The oil can is mine.
Losing this badly took real skill. Nobody will ever believe that.
He squints at it, then at me. We agree it isn't a drink token.
UsedWith: Token on the rail. Game on.
Dart one: the wall.
Dart two: a different wall.
Dart three is in the ocean. We're indoors. Nobody knows.
Flange is laughing so hard he goes for the showboat finish: three darts, one throw, behind the back.
His shoulder servo overheats with a noise like bacon achieving liftoff.
House rules: the overheated party forfeits the wager. The oil can is mine.
Losing this badly took real skill. Nobody will ever believe that.
He squints at it, then at me. We agree it isn't a drink token.
Smell: Hot bearings and confidence.

LookAt: A heavy back door. The sign says NO. Just NO. Punctuation costs extra.
Open: CLANG-CLANG. CLANG. CLANG-CLANG.
A slot opens. An eye looks at me. A voice says: 'Act Three, buddy.' The slot closes.
Everyone in this town has read the script except me.
Locked. From the sound of it, there's a knock-code. From the smell of it, I don't want to know.
Use: CLANG-CLANG. CLANG. CLANG-CLANG.
A slot opens. An eye looks at me. A voice says: 'Act Three, buddy.' The slot closes.
Everyone in this town has read the script except me.
Locked. From the sound of it, there's a knock-code. From the smell of it, I don't want to know.
Smell: Rust, secrets, and back-alley grease.

LookAt: Back through the gap, the docks. My ferry's still out there, doing modern art.
A steel door with no handle, no sign, and a brand-new red lamp.
The kind of door that's also a policy.
Open: Knock knock.
The slot opens. The same eye as the tavern. 'This is the BACK back door. STILL Act Three, buddy.'
This organization has remarkable message discipline.
Use: Knock knock.
The slot opens. The same eye as the tavern. 'This is the BACK back door. STILL Act Three, buddy.'
This organization has remarkable message discipline.
Move: The slot opens. The same eye as the tavern. 'This is the BACK back door. STILL Act Three, buddy.'
This organization has remarkable message discipline.
Smell: Rust and secrets. The house blend.

LookAt: Someone sprayed DYNAMO IS FINE. Crossed out. Under it, smaller: IT IS NOT.
Under that, very small: NO REFUNDS. The whole town, one wall.
Smell: Fresh paint over old paint over older paint. Sediment, with opinions.

LookAt: A municipal dumpster. The graffiti above appraises it at three stars.
Open: Inside: one rubber duck, seized. One manual for a better game. And a magnet on a string.
In a town held together by bolts, a magnet on a string is a license to print money.
The duck has seniority now. I'm not fighting the duck.
Use: Inside: one rubber duck, seized. One manual for a better game. And a magnet on a string.
In a town held together by bolts, a magnet on a string is a license to print money.
The duck has seniority now. I'm not fighting the duck.
Smell: Banana oil and municipal despair.

LookAt: A fire escape, and on it a Rustler with a spyglass, pointed at the docks.
He's taking notes. I'm in the notes.
TalkTo: I wave up at him. He writes that down too.
PickUp: The ladder's rusted shut. The Rustler shakes his head, slowly. Professional courtesy.
Use: The ladder's rusted shut. The Rustler shakes his head, slowly. Professional courtesy.

LookAt: A round little bot built entirely out of other bots, sorting screws by mood.
The stall out front says BACK IN FIVE OIL CHANGES. This is where he goes when he's back.
TalkTo: Rivet's the name, he says. Knows everything. First fact's free:
The funicular to Midtown takes ten bolts. Exact change. Gates can count — it's all they do.
Second fact costs a bolt. He can see I don't have one. He mentions that for free too.
Third freebie, out of pity: the harbor keeps everything this town drops, and that dumpster is — legally — his.
Nine bolts, I tell him. He appraises my pockets from four feet away. Correctly.
He proposes a riddle duel. His bolt against my official city notice. The alley has rules, apparently.
Rivet goes first: 'What has a hundred teeth and never bites?' A gear. In this town it's always a gear.
My turn. 'What winds down... but never winds up?'
He opens his mouth. He closes it. He looks uptown, at the Dynamo District.
He pays the bolt without a word. The best riddles aren't funny.
He says everything a bot needs is in that dumpster, and he says it as the dumpster's legal owner.
He eyes the magnet. He says the docks have a drain, the drain has a glint, and the glint has my name on it.
He waves. He's already sorting the bolts I fed the gate. I'm not asking how.
He nods at the gate. 'Ten bolts, exact change. It's all they do.'
Give: He appraises it, names a price, and the price is an insult. We're both happier keeping it.
UsedWith: He appraises it, names a price, and the price is an insult. We're both happier keeping it.
Smell: WD-40 and good information.

LookAt: Rivet's stock: pipes, springs, and a wheel from something that regrets losing it.
PickUp: Rivet clears his throat without having one. Inventory is sacred.
Use: Rivet clears his throat without having one. Inventory is sacred.
Smell: Rust. Vintage rust. Estate rust.

LookAt: Gate's open, car's lit. Midtown keeps glittering like it doesn't know I'm coming.
Act Two will still be uptown tomorrow. Probably. If the Dynamo holds.
A wrought-iron gate, a rail going up the cliff, and a sign: MIDTOWN. FARE, 10 BOLTS.
Uptown. Act Two. The future. It has a coin slot.
Open: Ten bolts in the hopper. It counts them twice. Trust died in this town years ago.
The gate ratchets up, and a little brass car rolls down to meet me, lit up like a promise.
Act Two. I can hear the neon from here.
The fare box wants ten bolts. I have pockets, and ambition.
I feed it nine bolts. It counts them, blinks NINE at me, and gives them all back.
Smug, for a gate. Rivet would know where a tenth bolt grows.
Use: Ten bolts in the hopper. It counts them twice. Trust died in this town years ago.
The gate ratchets up, and a little brass car rolls down to meet me, lit up like a promise.
Act Two. I can hear the neon from here.
The fare box wants ten bolts. I have pockets, and ambition.
I feed it nine bolts. It counts them, blinks NINE at me, and gives them all back.
Smug, for a gate. Rivet would know where a tenth bolt grows.
Smell: Ozone and altitude. The future smells expensive.

LookAt: The funicular, catching its breath. The fare down is free. The city knows which direction people give up in.
LEFTY'S SPARE PARTS. Everything slightly used, mostly left.
The window display is three gaskets and a dream.
Open: Closed for inventory. Through the glass I can see Lefty counting his own fingers.
Recounting.
Use: Closed for inventory. Through the glass I can see Lefty counting his own fingers.
Recounting.
Smell: Degreaser and optimism, in that order.

LookAt: The Oil Bar. Uptown filtered, triple distilled, quadruple priced.
There's a velvet rope. For robots. To stand behind. Voluntarily.
Open: A bouncer the size of a vending machine looks at my dock rust and invents a dress code.
I'm on the list, I tell him. He shows me the list. It's a picture of the door.
Use: A bouncer the size of a vending machine looks at my dock rust and invents a dress code.
I'm on the list, I tell him. He shows me the list. It's a picture of the door.
Smell: Single-origin crude. The good stuff. The unaffordable stuff.

LookAt: There it is. SPROCKET. On a marquee. In lights.
Two of the bulbs are out. It still feels like destiny.
Talent night. Tonight. No pressure. The marquee is the pressure.
The Grand Cog Theater. TALENT NITE, says the marquee, with a line left blank underneath.
A blank line on a marquee is the most dangerous sentence in show business.
Open: The doors open for performers and ticket holders. I'm neither. The doors can tell.
Doors open at showtime. The clerk said to go rehearse. The clerk has a point.
Use: The doors open for performers and ticket holders. I'm neither. The doors can tell.
Doors open at showtime. The clerk said to go rehearse. The clerk has a point.
Smell: Velvet, valve oil, and flop sweat. Showbiz.

LookAt: A box office with a clerk built into it. Literally. He IS the booth from the waist down.
TalkTo: Talent night, tonight, he says. Grand prize: an oil voucher and a backstage pass.
Backstage. Where Madame Voltina reads fortunes. And guards... things. He doesn't say what things.
Sign-up needs a stage name and an act. I have a name, and a key the size of my forearm.
He says, quote, that's more act than most.
He taps the sign-up slot and looks at my key. The slot is key-shaped, conceptually.
He says doors at eight, performers at seven, panicking at six. I'm ahead of schedule.
Give: I pitch the act: THE AMAZING SPROCKET WINDS HIMSELF UP. With a real key. On stage.
The clerk has seen everything twice, and he still blinks.
My name goes up on the marquee while I'm still holding the pen.
I keep the key. It's the act. It's also a legendary artifact, but mostly it's the act.
Already registered. One act per bot. House rules, and the house is a cog.
He slides it back. The slot only takes talent.
UsedWith: I pitch the act: THE AMAZING SPROCKET WINDS HIMSELF UP. With a real key. On stage.
The clerk has seen everything twice, and he still blinks.
My name goes up on the marquee while I'm still holding the pen.
I keep the key. It's the act. It's also a legendary artifact, but mostly it's the act.
Already registered. One act per bot. House rules, and the house is a cog.
He slides it back. The slot only takes talent.
Smell: Ticket stubs and stage fright.

LookAt: City Hall. The banner says NOTHING IS WRONG. It's brand new.
They replaced the old banner. The old one said the same thing, smaller.
Open: An aide opens the door exactly nine degrees. The Mayor is not seeing anyone.
Especially, he adds, anyone holding paper.
Use: An aide opens the door exactly nine degrees. The Mayor is not seeing anyone.
Especially, he adds, anyone holding paper.
UsedWith: I show the official notice through the gap. The aide identifies it as 'official' and 'correct'.
Then he closes the door eight of the nine degrees.
Somebody in there is afraid of this piece of paper. Noted. Filed. Act Two material.
The aide's eye appears in the gap, evaluates it, and declines on the city's behalf.
Smell: Fresh paint and old decisions.

LookAt: The Great Dynamo, up the hill, behind a fence with opinions.
Every bot on this island runs on that hum. Including me. The hum is flat by half a step.
Half a step doesn't sound like much. Neither does a clunk, until it's PERMANENT.
TalkTo: Hang in there, big fella. I've got one key and a lead on two more.
Smell: Ozone. The whole sky tastes like a battery up here.

LookAt: One genuine Scrap & Barrel drink token. Redeemable for one drink, zero refunds, unlimited regret.
Use: Those two things politely decline each other.
Gusket said Flange takes these as table stakes. At the dartboard.
Smell: Every hand in the tavern, in chronological order.

LookAt: Small-batch, dock-aged 10W-40. The good stuff.
Use: Those two things politely decline each other.
I'm not drinking it. It's medicine. For someone with a boom arm.
Smell: Notes of machine shop, with a long viscous finish.

LookAt: A brass wind-up key the size of my forearm. One of three.
The legends say so. The legends are a stencil on a crate. Still counts.
Use: Those two things politely decline each other.
It wants a keyhole the size of a manhole. I'll know it when I see it.
Smell: Brass and consequence.

LookAt: A magnet on a string. The harbor's least regulated financial instrument.
Use: Those two things politely decline each other.
It wants something ferrous. The storm drain on the docks was full of glint.
Smell: It smells of attraction. Strictly professional.

LookAt: Nine bolts, fresh from the harbor. The fare to Midtown is ten.
Nine is the loneliest number that isn't a fare.
Use: Those two things politely decline each other.
I count them again. Still nine. Counting is not the bottleneck.
Smell: Brine and compound interest.

LookAt: The bolt Rivet paid out on the riddle. It looks exactly like a bolt that used to be his.
Use: Those two things politely decline each other.
With the other nine, that's a fare. The gate does the math. It's all gates do.
Smell: Faintly of riddles.
